sn22.59:1.1: At one time, the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Bārāṇasī, in the Deer Park at Isipatana.
sn22.59:1.2: There the *Bhagavā* addressed the *bhikkhus* of the group of five:
sn22.59:10.1: Whatever *viññāṇa* there is—past, future, or present; internal or external; coarse or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near—all *viññāṇa* should be seen as it truly is with right discernment [*sammappaññāya*] thus: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’
sn22.59:11.1: Seeing thus, *bhikkhus*, a learned noble disciple becomes disenchanted with form [*rūpasmimpi*], disenchanted with *vedanā*, disenchanted with *saññā*, disenchanted with *saṅkhāras*, and disenchanted with *viññāṇa*.
sn22.59:11.2: Being disenchanted, they become dispassionate; through dispassion, they are liberated. When liberated, there is the knowledge, ‘Liberated.’
sn22.59:11.3: They understand: ‘Birth is ended, the holy life has been lived, what had to be done has been done; there is no more of this state.’”
sn22.59:12.2: Satisfied, the *bhikkhus* of the group of five delighted in the *Bhagavā*’s statement.
sn22.59:12.3: And while this exposition was being spoken, the minds [*cittāni*] of the *bhikkhus* of the group of five were liberated from the *āsavas* through non-*upādāna*.
sn22.59:2.2: For if this form [*rūpaṁ*], *bhikkhus*, were self, this form [*rūpaṁ*] would not lead to affliction, and it would be possible to have it of form [*rūpe*]:
sn22.59:2.3: ‘Let my form [*rūpaṁ*] be thus; let my form [*rūpaṁ*] not be thus.’
sn22.59:2.4: But because, *bhikkhus*, form [*rūpaṁ*] is not-self [*anattā*], therefore form [*rūpaṁ*] leads to affliction, and it is not possible to have it of form [*rūpe*]:
sn22.59:2.5: ‘Let my form [*rūpaṁ*] be thus; let my form [*rūpaṁ*] not be thus.’
sn22.59:3.2: For if this *vedanā*, *bhikkhus*, were self, this *vedanā* would not lead to affliction, and it would be possible to have it of *vedanā*:
sn22.59:3.3: ‘Let my *vedanā* be thus; let my *vedanā* not be thus.’
sn22.59:3.4: But because, *bhikkhus*, *vedanā* is not-self [*anattā*], therefore *vedanā* leads to affliction, and it is not possible to have it of *vedanā*:
sn22.59:3.5: ‘Let my *vedanā* be thus; let my *vedanā* not be thus.’
sn22.59:4.3: For if these *saṅkhāras*, *bhikkhus*, were self, these *saṅkhāras* would not lead to affliction, and it would be possible to have it of *saṅkhāras*:
sn22.59:4.4: ‘Let my *saṅkhāras* be thus; let my *saṅkhāras* not be thus.’
sn22.59:4.5: But because, *bhikkhus*, *saṅkhāras* are not-self [*anattā*], therefore *saṅkhāras* lead to affliction, and it is not possible to have it of *saṅkhāras*:
sn22.59:4.6: ‘Let my *saṅkhāras* be thus; let my *saṅkhāras* not be thus.’
sn22.59:5.2: For if this *viññāṇa*, *bhikkhus*, were self, this *viññāṇa* would not lead to affliction, and it would be possible to have it of *viññāṇa*:
sn22.59:5.3: ‘Let my *viññāṇa* be thus; let my *viññāṇa* not be thus.’
sn22.59:5.4: But because, *bhikkhus*, *viññāṇa* is not-self [*anattā*], therefore *viññāṇa* leads to affliction, and it is not possible to have it of *viññāṇa*:
sn22.59:5.5: ‘Let my *viññāṇa* be thus; let my *viññāṇa* not be thus.’
sn22.59:6.14: “But what is impermanent [*aniccaṁ*], is that *dukkha* or *sukha*?”
sn22.59:6.16: “But what is impermanent [*aniccaṁ*], *dukkha*, and subject to change—would it be fitting to regard it thus:
sn22.59:6.17: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?”
sn22.59:6.4: “But what is impermanent [*aniccaṁ*], is that *dukkha* or *sukha*?”
sn22.59:6.6: “But what is impermanent [*aniccaṁ*], *dukkha*, and subject to change—would it be fitting to regard it thus:
sn22.59:6.7: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?”
sn22.59:7.1: “Therefore, *bhikkhus*, whatever form [*rūpaṁ*] there is—past, future, or present; internal or external; coarse or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near—all form [*rūpaṁ*] should be seen as it truly is with right discernment [*sammappaññāya*] thus: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’
sn22.59:8.1: Whatever *vedanā* there is—past, future, or present; internal or external … far or near—all *vedanā* should be seen as it truly is with right discernment [*sammappaññāya*] thus: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’
sn22.59:9.2: whatever *saṅkhāras* there are—past, future, or present; internal or external … far or near—all *saṅkhāras* should be seen as it truly is with right discernment [*sammappaññāya*] thus: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’
